cards.back: Base (B) — control, Collector (C) — output, Emitter (E) — common terminal. IE = IC + IB always.

cards.front: NPN vs PNP: how to tell from symbol?
cards.back: NPN: emitter arrow points OUT ('Not Pointing iN'). PNP: arrow points IN. Arrow shows conventional current direction.
β = IC/IB = DC current gain (also called hFE). Typical range: 50-200. Small IB controls large IC.
α = IC/IE = β/(β+1). For β=100: α=0.99. Nearly all emitter current reaches the collector.
BE junction: forward biased (VBE ≈ 0.7V). BC junction: reverse biased. Result: IC = βIB (amplification).
Both BE and BC forward biased. VCE ≈ 0.2V. Transistor acts as closed switch. Cannot amplify.
Both BE and BC reverse biased. IC ≈ 0, IB ≈ 0. Transistor acts as open switch.
To supply many majority carriers (electrons in NPN) for injection into the thin, lightly doped base.

cards.front: BJT in active region = what model?
cards.back: Current-controlled current source (CCCS): BE = diode (VBE≈0.7V), collector = current source (IC = βIB).
IE = (β+1)·IB. Since β >> 1, IE ≈ IC. Example: β=100 → IE = 101·IB.
